planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped trees in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–November. Natchez is among the largest and fastest-growing National Arboretum crepe myrtle introductions, reaching 20–30 feet at maturity — a true tree-sized specimen, not a large shrub. Do NOT 'crape murder' (top with flush cuts) — this practice produces disfigured trees with weak, gnarled tops that look unnatural and structurally inferior. Instead, remove only crossing branches, suckers, and dead wood in late winter. Natchez's disease resistance (especially powdery mildew and Cercospora leaf spot) is among the best of all crepe myrtle selections, a direct result of the Japanese L. fauriei parentage in the hybrid.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Natchez Crepe Myrtle thrives in summer heat and drought, requiring supplemental water only during establishment and extended dry periods once mature; Natchez is the flagship white-flowered introduction from the National Arboretum's landmark crepe myrtle breeding program, released in 1978 as one of the first L. indica x fauriei hybrids that combined the large, colorful flowers of L. indica with the disease resistance and refined exfoliating bark of the Japanese L. fauriei species. The extraordinary, exfoliating cinnamon-to-chestnut bark that peels to reveal cream, tan, and mahogany underlayers is arguably the most beautiful bark texture of any tree in the Southern landscape — providing striking ornamental interest throughout the fall and winter months long after the white flower clusters have dropped. The pure white flower clusters appear in midsummer (June–August), covering the entire canopy with massive, frothy white blooms before the foliage turns bronze-orange-red in fall. Natchez's combination of spectacular summer bloom, beautiful autumn color, exceptional winter bark, disease resistance, and adaptability to the heat and drought of zones 6–9 has made it one of the most widely planted ornamental trees in the American South for over four decades.
sources: USDA National Arboretum — Natchez Crepe Myrtle Introduction
North Carolina State University Extension — Lagerstroemia National Arboretum Hybrids
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Crepe Myrtle Selection for Gulf South

planting.note: Plant container-grown or rooted cutting transplants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Petite Negra is naturally dwarf, remaining under 5 feet without pruning — ideal for container growing, patio culture, and small garden spaces where standard fig trees (8–15 feet) would be too large. In zone 9 it produces two crops: a small breba (early) crop in June–July on previous season's wood, and the main crop in August–October. The dark purple to near-black skin and sweet, amber-to-red flesh distinguish the fruit visually from brown or green-skinned fig varieties. In containers, repot every 2–3 years and feed regularly with balanced fertilizer during the growing season for maximum fruiting.

directions: Plant in full sun to light partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — figs are adaptable to a wide range of soils but require good drainage and at least 8 hours of sun for maximum fruit production; Petite Negra is a naturally compact, dwarf fig cultivar producing small, round to pear-shaped fruits with dark purple to nearly black skin and amber to red, rich, sweet flesh — delivering the characteristic fresh fig flavor at a manageable plant scale that makes fresh fig production accessible to gardeners with limited space. The dwarf habit (3–5 feet in the ground, 2–3 feet in containers) is the primary distinguishing feature: Petite Negra produces full-sized, high-quality fruit on plants small enough to grow as container specimens on patios, to fit in small garden beds, or to overwinter indoors in containers in marginally cold climates. Container-grown fig culture allows gardeners outside the traditional fig belt (zones 7–10) to grow figs by moving plants indoors or into garages during winter; zone 9 gardeners can grow Petite Negra in the ground as a permanent, productive perennial shrub or in large containers for patio display. The self-fruitful nature (no cross-pollination required) and rapid return to bearing (often in the first or second year) make Petite Negra one of the most immediately rewarding fruit plants available.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Dwarf Fig Varieties for Florida Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Container Fig Production for Gulf South
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Compact Fig Cultivars for Small Spaces

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Crossandra is a tropical perennial that grows as a tender perennial or treated as an annual in zone 9 — established plants may survive mild winters in sheltered, well-mulched locations but should be considered annual in exposed sites. Crossandra tolerates and actually prefers partial shade to deep shade in zone 9's summer, where full sun exposure combined with summer heat can stress the plant and reduce flower quality. The salmon-orange fan-shaped flower clusters are produced continuously from late spring through fall without deadheading. Excellent performance in humid, hot conditions makes Crossandra one of the most reliable partial-shade flowering plants for Gulf South summers.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun (morning sun, afternoon shade preferred) in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moist soil — Crossandra thrives in warm, humid, partially shaded conditions that mimic its native South Asian forest understory habitat; Orange Marmalade is an improved cultivar of Crossandra (Crossandra infundibuliformis), a compact tropical perennial producing fan-shaped clusters of overlapping, distinctively asymmetrical salmon-orange to coral flowers on short spikes above glossy, dark green, undulating leaves. The flower shape is unusual and attractive — the individual flowers have five rounded petals arranged in a slightly asymmetrical fan, a characteristic flower structure of the Crossandra genus that creates a layered, tiered appearance on the flower spikes. Crossandra's most valuable landscape quality is its continuous production of attractive blooms through the heat, humidity, and partial shade of zone 9 summers — conditions that limit or stop flowering in many ornamental annuals and tender perennials. While impatiens are the default shade annual for many Gulf South gardeners, Crossandra handles the heat-humidity combination of summer significantly better than most Impatiens walleriana varieties, making it the more reliable summer shade bloomer when temperatures remain in the 90s for weeks at a time.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Crossandra infundibuliformis for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Perennials for Shaded Gulf South Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Shade-Tolerant Annuals and Tender Perennials for Zone 9

planting.note: Plant outdoors after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May and bring containers indoors before temperatures drop below 35°F in fall. In zone 10–11 Schefflera arboricola can remain outdoors year-round as a landscape shrub reaching 8–15 feet. In zone 9 it is best managed as a container specimen or porch plant — moved outdoors for the warm season and overwintered indoors. Prune leggy stems in spring to maintain bushy compact form; Schefflera responds well to pruning. Remove any all-green reversion shoots on variegated plants immediately to prevent them from overtaking the cream-variegated growth.

directions: Plant in bright indirect light to partial shade outdoors — Trinette Schefflera thrives in shade conditions where direct sun is absent or minimal, making it one of the most useful large foliage plants for covered patios, deep porches, and shaded landscape settings; Trinette is a variegated cultivar of Dwarf Umbrella Plant (Schefflera arboricola), producing the characteristic glossy, palmate, compound leaves of schefflera in a green-and-cream to yellow-green variegated form that provides significantly more ornamental interest than the plain green species. Each palmately compound leaf is composed of 7–9 leaflets radiating from a central point in the classic umbrella arrangement that gives the plant its common name, with the Trinette cultivar's cream-yellow variegation adding a bright, light-catching quality to the foliage that works especially well in shaded settings. As a patio or porch container plant in zone 9, Trinette Schefflera fills the role of a large, architectural foliage plant for shaded outdoor living spaces — providing a tropical, lush texture that transforms a covered porch or shaded patio into an outdoor room with lush, jungle-like plant material. The size and bold foliage make it a genuine statement plant for large containers on shaded patios, and it overwinters successfully as an indoor houseplant in bright indirect light.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Schefflera arboricola for Florida Landscapes and Containers
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical Foliage Plants for Zone 9 Containers
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade Container Plants for Gulf South Patios

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants after frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant February–May or September–October. Sprengeri Fern grows from tuberous roots that allow it to survive periods of drought and some winter cold. In zone 9 it behaves as a semi-evergreen perennial, surviving mild winters with minimal damage and resprouting vigorously after hard freezes kill the above-ground stems. The trailing, cascading stems make it excellent in hanging baskets, window boxes, and container plantings where the growth can hang freely. Small, fragrant white flowers appear on established plants followed by red berries — attractive but mildly toxic if ingested. Can become invasive where birds drop seeds in warm, frost-free areas.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Sprengeri Fern adapts to a wide light range but produces the most lush, dense growth in consistent partial shade with regular moisture; Sprengeri Fern is the most widely grown ornamental asparagus cultivar — a trailing, cascading, arching plant with airy, needle-like cladodes (the green, needle-like modified stems that function as leaves) clothing long, flexible stems that naturally arch and cascade 2–4 feet from their point of origin. Despite the common name, Sprengeri is not a true fern but an asparagus relative (Asparagus densiflorus) native to South Africa, sharing only the fine, feathery texture that gives it a fern-like appearance. The light, airy, bright green texture of cascading Sprengeri stems is one of the most versatile elements in both container design and cut flower arranging — the stems add a loose, naturalistic, fine-textured complement to virtually any floral combination, and the sprays remain fresh for an extended period after cutting. As a container plant for shaded patios and porches in zone 9, Sprengeri's cascading habit and fine texture provide an essential design element that trails gracefully from the edges of containers while tolerating the heat and partial shade conditions of covered outdoor spaces.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Asparagus densiflorus 'Sprengeri' Landscape Use
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Ornamental Asparagus for Gulf South Containers
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Foliage Plants for Zone 9 Outdoor Living Spaces
